key_insight: A machine learning pipeline can separate muon and hadron tracks in the GRAPES-3 muon telescope, improving particle identification in cosmic-ray air-shower events.
engineering_relevance: Accurate muon-versus-hadron classification helps reduce detector background and improves estimation of primary cosmic-ray properties from GRAPES-3 measurements.
method: Simulated CORSIKA proton showers were passed through a Geant4 detector model to generate labeled detector-hit data.
Single-particle classifiers were tested, including decision trees, random forests, neural networks, and XGBoost.
For multiparticle events, a graph neural network with edge convolution classified detector hits, followed by a Dynamic Reduction Network for particle-count regression.
evidence_signal: On single-particle classification, XGBoost achieved the best reported accuracy at 88.7% on the simulated dataset.
watch_item: Results are based on simulated proton showers and detector modeling, so performance may differ on real mixed-composition data and in operational noise conditions.
paper_title: Machine learning pipeline for identifying tracks of muons and hadrons at GRAPES-3 muon telescope
